article_title: The Pre-Modern Baseline (1900–1919) — The Moral Decline of America
thesis: The Victorian moral facade of 1900-1919 was a brittle, constraint-based pseudo-coherence that masked deep internal fragmentation and was structurally eroded by progressive centralization, making it the baseline against which all subsequent moral decline is measured.

primary_nodes.evidence: The article provides a detailed empirical case study of social coherence at the national level during the pre-modern era, showing its dependence on external constraints rather than internal consensus.

The article shows that the apparent high coherence of the Victorian era was forced by external constraints, not by self-generated increase, and collapsed when constraints were removed, supporting the Moral Second Law.
The article assumes that social structures carry information about moral order and that measuring coherence requires distinguishing stable vs. unstable configurations.
The baseline analysis presupposes the existence of a measurable societal state (1900-1919) as a reference point for decline.

key_vulnerability: If the data on marital disruption rates, hidden vice, or the degree of constraint-based stability are shown to be inaccurate or misinterpreted, the argument that pre-modern coherence was brittle collapses.

framework_coverage.chain_position: Mid-chain (nodes 25-100) with applications to scale nodes (178-179)
